cataloging_subject.label: Extracts from the First Version of Hardyng's Chronicle
cataloging_subject.scope_note: C. L. Kingsford's October 1912 editorial selection of three discontiguous passages from John Hardyng's first-version Chronicle: the dedication and most of the prologue, the praise of Henry V, and a composite conclusion comprising praise of Robert Umfraville, counsel on common profit, Hardyng's grievance, and two final envoy stanzas. The cataloged unit includes Kingsford's short preface and tailnote as explicit editorial components but does not turn them into Hardyng's authorship. It excludes the intervening Scotland-itinerary chapter summarized but not transcribed on p. 753, the rest of Hardyng's Chronicle, the physical manuscript, the journal issue as a carrier, the U-M encoding, and the repository XML as distinct layers.

catalog_summary.title: Extracts from the First Version of Hardyng's Chronicle
catalog_summary.author: John Hardyng
catalog_summary.date: First-version Chronicle completed in 1457; edited selection published 1 October 1912
catalog_summary.region: England; exact place of composition is not securely established; the sole manuscript was produced in eastern England

titles.value: Extracts from the First Version of Hardyng's Chronicle

assertion.notes: A discovery short title, not historical title wording.

agents.name: John Hardyng
notes: Author of the Middle English Chronicle passages; not author of Kingsford's modern editorial prose.

agents.name: Charles Lethbridge Kingsford
notes: Selector, transcriber, editor, and author of the 1912 article's modern preface and tailnote.

scope.description: All seven modeled medieval verse components
The selection, arrangement, textual presentation, and notes in the October 1912 article
Transcription from the sole first-version witness, British Library Lansdowne MS 204
Modern English article framing, historical glosses, and comparison notes

date_statements.display: Composed and revised principally in the 1450s; completed in 1457

scope.description: The selected passages as parts of Hardyng's first-version Chronicle

assertion.notes: The 1450–1457 range is an operational summary of current scholarship, not a claim that every selected stanza was first drafted in that span. Kingsford argued that the praise of Henry V may predate the loss of Normandy, but that passage-specific inference is not normalized as a separate firm date.

scope.description: Kingsford's selection, transcription, arrangement, preface, and notes
Hardyng's English political and historiographical context

assertion.notes: The eastern-English production of Lansdowne 204 is a witness fact, not proof of the exact place where Hardyng composed every selected passage.

scope.description: Kingsford's modern article framing and notes

form_statements.description: An edited selection combining 546 Middle English verse lines in seventy-eight seven-line stanzas with Kingsford's prose preface, tailnote, arguments, and annotations.

assertion.notes: The mixed label describes the cataloged 1912 selection. Hardyng's intrinsic selected text is verse; modern prose does not alter the form of the medieval Chronicle.

subject_statements.label: Anglo-Scottish relations, claims, geography, and military planning

assertion.notes: This describes the selection's historical claims and proposed violence; it does not endorse them. The route chapter itself is summarized but omitted.

subject_statements.label: Commemoration and praise of Henry V
Authorial grievance, service, injury, and hoped-for reward

summaries.value: Kingsford selected three politically and autobiographically distinctive passages from Hardyng's first-version Chronicle. The dedication and proem present the book to Henry VI while rehearsing Hardyng's Scottish evidence and unrewarded service; the praise of Henry V turns royal commemoration into counsel about peace and law; and the conclusion praises Robert Umfraville, urges the king to defend common profit, renews Hardyng's grievance, and closes with two envoy stanzas. The selection is a direct transcription from the sole first-version witness, not a multi-witness critical edition.

The XML contains one prose editorial preface and three numbered verse selections. Its 78 LG elements each hold seven lines, for 546 verse lines: Introduction 22 stanzas/154 lines (Dedication 4/28; Proem 18/126), Praise of Henry V 14/98, and Conclusion 42/294 (Umfraville 15/105; common-profit counsel 14/98; grievance 11/77; quoted envoy 2/14). Kingsford's prose tailnote summarizes but does not transcribe the intervening Scotland chapter.

The manuscript run is discontiguous: dedication/proem at ff. 2v–4r; praise of Henry V at ff. 216r–217v; conclusion main run at ff. 220r–223v; and final envoy at current f. 226r. The article omits the text between the third stanza on f. 223v and the final two stanzas on f. 226r. Kingsford prints the envoy locator as f. 226v, but the current BL viewer shows f. 226v occupied by the Scotland map and the envoy on f. 226r.

Kingsford and the XML print 154 lines for the dedication and proem. Simpson and Peverley recover a further erased seven-line stanza, Prol.155–161, under ultraviolet light at the bottom of f. 4r. The complete manuscript prologue is therefore 161 lines, while this historical 1912 selection legitimately remains 154 lines.

extent.description: Parent-aware encoding metrics. The three top-level numbered verse selections contain 78 seven-line stanzas and 546 lines. Seven leaf-level verse components partition that total without overlap; two prose-only editorial components are counted separately. Four unnumbered manuscript argument or rubric paragraphs are embedded within the numbered selections, and one further rubric for the omitted Scotland chapter is quoted inside Kingsford's tailnote; these paratexts are not additional numbered works or prose items. Introduction and Conclusion are aggregate parent containers and are not added again to the leaf-level item totals. Eleven modeled content parts include those two aggregate parents.

content_parts.label: Kingsford's editorial preface
content_parts.summary: One prose paragraph identifies the selection as a promised continuation, calls the three passages distinctive, names the sole Lansdowne 204 copy, and explains their autobiographical and political interest; a signed closing follows.

content_parts.label: Dedication to Henry VI
content_parts.summary: Four rhyme-royal stanzas present the book to Henry VI, Margaret of Anjou, and Prince Edward and invoke English sovereignty over Scotland.

content_parts.label: Proem
content_parts.summary: Eighteen printed stanzas state Hardyng's grievance, describe his collection and presentation of evidence for English sovereignty over Scotland, and petition Henry VI for the reward promised by Henry V.

locators.value: printed pp. 741–743; issue scan PDF pp. 121–123

notes: Kingsford restores damage in three stanzas partly from a parallel passage later in the same manuscript.
The erased nineteenth stanza recovered by Simpson and Peverley is outside this historical selection's encoded extent.

content_parts.label: II. The Praise of King Henry V
content_parts.summary: Fourteen stanzas lament Henry V, praise his governance, peacekeeping, and French campaigns, and turn that example into direct counsel to Henry VI against disorder and for recovery in France.

locators.value: printed pp. 744–746; issue scan PDF pp. 124–126
f. 216r, final stanza, through f. 217v, first stanza; canvases 437–440
BODY/DIV1[@TYPE='poem']/DIV2[@TYPE='paean']

notes: The XML's ARGUMENT/P before the verse is a manuscript-derived descriptive rubric within this numbered selection, not a separate numbered work section.
Kingsford notes that two stanzas have slight-variation parallels in Ellis's later-version edition, p. 388; this does not make the later version a base witness.

content_parts.label: III. The Conclusion
content_parts.summary: Aggregate parent for forty continuously transcribed stanzas at ff. 220r–223v, Kingsford's prose tailnote summarizing an omitted chapter, and two final envoy stanzas quoted from current f. 226r.

locators.value: printed pp. 746–753; issue scan PDF pp. 126–133
main run f. 220r, final stanza, through f. 223v, first three stanzas, canvases 445–452; envoy current f. 226r, final two stanzas, canvas 457
BODY/DIV1[@TYPE='poem']/DIV2[@TYPE='conclusion']

notes: Aggregate metrics are not added again to the extent total.
The omitted run after the third stanza on f. 223v continues through most of f. 226r; only the final two stanzas return in the selection.

content_parts.label: Commendation of Sir Robert Umfraville
content_parts.summary: Fifteen stanzas commemorate Hardyng's lord Robert Umfraville as a just, peacekeeping, generous, and militarily accomplished northern knight.

locators.value: printed pp. 746–748; issue scan PDF pp. 126–128
f. 220r, final stanza, through f. 221v, second stanza; canvases 445–448

content_parts.label: Counsel on common profit, peace, and law
content_parts.summary: Fourteen stanzas use Umfraville's example to urge Henry VI to restrain riot, maintenance, and local oppression, preserve peace and law, and secure national unity before foreign campaigning.

locators.value: printed pp. 748–751; issue scan PDF pp. 128–131
f. 221v, third stanza, through f. 222v, fourth stanza; canvases 448–450
DIV2[@TYPE='conclusion']/DIV3[1]/LG[1] through LG[14]

content_parts.label: Hardyng's grievance and petition for reward
content_parts.summary: Eleven stanzas describe Hardyng's intelligence work in Scotland, his claimed costs and injury, Henry V's promise, Henry VI's frustrated grant of Geddington, and the unrewarded evidence offered to the crown.

locators.value: printed pp. 751–753; issue scan PDF pp. 131–133
f. 222v, final two stanzas, through f. 223v, first three stanzas; canvases 450–452
DIV2[@TYPE='conclusion']/DIV3[2]/LG[1] through LG[11]

content_parts.label: Kingsford's tailnote on the omitted Scotland chapter
content_parts.summary: Modern prose summarizes the omitted chapter about conquering Scotland and its map, points to Ellis's later-version pages 423–429, and introduces the two final envoy stanzas.

locators.value: printed p. 753; issue scan PDF p. 133

notes: The summarized itinerary is not counted as encoded medieval text.

content_parts.label: Envoy
content_parts.summary: Two final rhyme-royal stanzas ask Henry VI to accept the book and service, consider Hardyng's loss and injury, and excuse his limited literary ability.

locators.value: printed p. 753; issue scan PDF p. 133

notes: Kingsford notes that the stanzas recur with alterations at the beginning of the later-version Excusacion, Ellis p. 420.

related_works.title: The Chronicle of John Hardyng (first version)
notes: All medieval verse in CME00119 is selected from this work; the selection is not a complete representation of it. DIMEV 1174 groups first- and later-version witnesses in one Chronicle-level record.

related_works.title: The Chronicle of John Hardyng (second version)
notes: Kingsford cites only passage-level later-version parallels through Ellis's 1812 edition; the second version is not a second base witness for this selection.

evidence.citation: General Corpus / Kyvero-Press CME source XML, CME/source/CME_phase_3/CME00119.xml, pinned CME commit e1d77c88ca561be924b2fedc54dd474be2e7e49c.
evidence.locator: TEMPHEAD revision; IDG; TEXT/BODY; complete preface and poem hierarchy
evidence.summary: Primary local evidence for the CME identifiers, source languages, article title and preface, three selected passages, printed-page breaks 740–753, notes, and 2016 MURP structural/proofing revision.

evidence.citation: Structural and boundary audit of the pinned CME00119 XML artifact.
evidence.locator: Counts and nesting of DIV1, DIV2, DIV3, HEAD, P, ARGUMENT, TAILNOTE, PB, LG, L, and NOTE
evidence.summary: Inspection found two DIV1, three DIV2, four DIV3, six HEAD, fourteen PB, eleven NOTE, seven P, seventy-eight LG, and 546 L elements. Introduction is 22 stanzas/154 lines (4/28 dedication; 18/126 proem), Praise is 14/98, and Conclusion is 42/294 (15/105 commendation; 14/98 counsel; 11/77 grievance; 2/14 envoy nested in the tailnote).

evidence.citation: University of Michigan Library, Corpus of Middle English Prose and Verse, CME00119, Extracts from the first version of Hardyng's Chronicle.
evidence.summary: Official item evidence for the stable identifier, title, Hardyng and Kingsford responsibility display, public text, current No Copyright–United States statement, and exact displayed contents hierarchy.

evidence.citation: C. L. Kingsford, 'Extracts from the First Version of Hardyng's Chronicle,' The English Historical Review 27, no. 108 (October 1912): 740–753.
evidence.summary: Immediate-source evidence for exact title, author/editor, publication, one-witness selection statement, component order, transcribed text, damage restoration, historical annotations, later-version comparisons, omitted Scotland chapter, and printed envoy locator.

evidence.citation: Oxford Academic, Extracts from the First Version of Hardyng's Chronicle, DOI 10.1093/ehr/XXVII.CVIII.740.
evidence.summary: Current publisher metadata confirms C. L. Kingsford, exact article title, The English Historical Review volume XXVII issue CVIII, October 1912, pages 740–753, publication date 1 October 1912, and DOI.

evidence.citation: C. L. Kingsford, 'The First Version of Hardyng's Chronicle,' The English Historical Review 27, no. 107 (July 1912): 462–482.
evidence.summary: The preceding analytical installment identifies Lansdowne 204 as the sole first-version witness, discusses composition and presentation, maps the distinctive closing sequence, and explains why Kingsford would publish the three extracts in the next issue.

evidence.citation: British Library Archives and Manuscripts Catalogue, Lansdowne MS 204, John Hardyng, Chronicle (first version), record 040-002073484.
evidence.summary: Current official evidence for the exact shelfmark and sole first-version status; content loci; 1440–1450 catalog range; eastern-English origin; parchment extent; dimensions; at least two hands; Middle English and Latin; decoration; map; physical access; and complete digitized-content link.

evidence.citation: British Library IIIF Presentation 3 manifest and working image boundaries for Lansdowne MS 204.
evidence.summary: Audit confirms a complete 474-canvas public object from front cover through spine and maps the selection to canvases 10–13, 437–440, 445–452, and 457. Visual inspection shows the envoy on current f. 226r/canvas 457 and the map on f. 226v/canvas 458, correcting the side implied by Kingsford's printed locator.

evidence.citation: James Simpson and Sarah Peverley, eds., John Hardyng's Chronicle, Edited from British Library MS Lansdowne 204, Volume I (Kalamazoo: Medieval Institute Publications, 2015).
evidence.locator: PDF pp. 1–28, especially manuscript description and editorial procedures; PDF pp. 30–33, Dedication and Prologue; PDF p. 5, ultraviolet figure
evidence.summary: Current scholarly evidence for first-version identity, 1457 completion, work themes, sole witness, manuscript production and hands, modern editorial method, exact prologue lineation, and the erased stanza Prol.155–161 recovered under ultraviolet light. Volume I contains only the dedication, prologue, and Books 1–3, not Kingsford's closing extracts.

evidence.citation: John Hardyng, The Chronicle of Iohn Hardyng, ed. Henry Ellis (London, 1812), complete Harvard–Google scan supplied by Internet Archive.
evidence.summary: Directly checked comparison source for the later-version passages cited by Kingsford: Henry V parallels at p. 388, altered envoy at pp. 420–421, and the Scotland route at pp. 423–429. It is not the base text of CME00119.

evidence.citation: Digital Index of Middle English Verse, source-data release v1.0, record 1174 / IMEV 710 and witness wit-1174-10.
evidence.locator: data/Records.xml record-1174 and wit-1174-10; data/Bibliography.xml Kingsford1912; LICENSE
evidence.summary: DIMEV v1 identifies Hardyng, the Chronicle, seven-line ababbcc form, IMEV/NIMEV 710, Lansdowne 204 witness ff. 5–226, and Kingsford pp. 740–753 as extracts only. Its one Chronicle-level record groups twenty witness entries across first and later versions; a commented-out dedication entry is not an active independent record. Its bibliography incorrectly pairs the title The First Version of Hardyng's Chronicle with pp. 740–753, so OUP and the article itself control the exact title.

open_questions.question: What initial keying source, date, transcription method, correction history, and bulk-source package preceded the repository XML's documented 2016 MURP review?
next_steps: Ask the University of Michigan Library for item-level production metadata and any preserved source package for CME00119.
Compare any supplied source with the October 1912 issue and the checked-in XML without treating the 2016 review as the initial encoding date.

open_questions.question: Why does Kingsford locate the final two stanzas at f. 226v when the current British Library foliation and viewer place them on f. 226r and the map on f. 226v?
next_steps: Ask the British Library whether the manuscript was refoliated or whether Kingsford's side designation is simply an error.
Retain both the historical printed locator and current verified folio/canvas in discovery data.

open_questions.question: What is the current publication status of the promised later METS volume containing Book 7 and the complete modern critical text corresponding to Kingsford's praise and conclusion extracts?
next_steps: Ask METS or the editors for the status of Volume II and whether a stable critical text or line mapping for Book 7 is available.
Do not imply that the cached 2015 Volume I contains the Kingsford conclusion merely because its landing page describes the wider multivolume project as a complete edition.

notes: Lansdowne MS 204 is the sole first-version witness. Ellis 1812 is cited for limited later-version parallels and the omitted itinerary, not silently promoted to a second base witness.
The cataloged military and sovereignty claims are historical subject matter and are not endorsed.
All stanza and line totals are reproducible encoding metrics from the pinned XML. Parent aggregates are explicitly non-additive.

The current BL catalog's 1440–1450 manuscript range, the modern edition's 1450s production account, and the 1457 completion/presentation claim are kept as distinct assertions rather than forced into one date.
